blocks.text: Among those foremost in taking the revelations received by Joseph Smith to the world were members of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles. In 1839, fulfilling a revelation Joseph had received the previous year, they left Nauvoo “to go over the great waters” of the Atlantic Ocean to preach the gospel and “bear record.” As they traveled eastward, they thought about the need for scriptures in their missionary work.

On December 22, 1839, the Prophet’s brother Hyrum responded to a request from Parley P. Pratt, who sought permission to publish the Book of Mormon in New York. Hyrum, who answered because Joseph was away at the time, acknowledged a shortage of scriptures “throughout the Union” but reinforced the need to publish scriptures near Church headquarters “under the immediate inspection of Joseph and his Counselors, so that no one may be chargeable with any mistakes that may occur.

“I want the books we print here should be a standard to all nations in which they may be printed, and to all tongues into which the same may be translated,” Hyrum instructed. “The above observations will apply to the book of Doctrine and Covenants, Hymn Book, etc., which publications I long to see flowing through the land like a stream, imparting knowledge, intelligence, and joy to all who shall drink at the stream.” Though wanting American editions of scriptures to be published in Nauvoo, Hyrum recognized the need for scriptures to be published overseas.

On May 1, 1840, Orson Hyde and John E. Page wrote from Columbus, Ohio, to Joseph Smith as they thought of the “great work to be done in Germany.” They inquired, “Should we consider it necessary to translate the entire Book of Mormon into German, and Doctrine and Covenants too; are we or are we not at liberty to do so[?]”

Six days later, after some of the missionaries reached England, Brigham Young wrote to the Prophet for permission to publish the Book of Mormon there. But he also posed “another question,” asking, “Is the book of Doctrine and Covenants to be printed just as it is now to go to the nations of the earth, and shall we give it to them as quick as we can or what shall we do[?]”

On May 14, Joseph answered Elders Hyde and Page, granting permission for them to publish Church books—including the Doctrine and Covenants—overseas, provided they took care to secure copyrights in the Prophet’s name. Joseph had already been a victim of copyright piracy, and he knew the importance to the Church of legally protecting the scriptures.

Being much farther away from Nauvoo, Brigham Young waited much longer to receive an answer to his questions about scripture publication. Moved by the Spirit, he went forward with publishing the Book of Mormon and a hymnbook in England but waited to go ahead with the Doctrine and Covenants.

Not hearing anything for four months after his first inquiry, Brigham Young again wrote to Joseph Smith. He asked: “Shall we print the Doctrine & Covenants here or not? or will the D. & C. be printed & go to the nations, as it now is or not? or will it be reviewed and printed for the nations?”

In October, Lorenzo Snow arrived in England from the United States, carrying a letter from Joseph Smith that had become smudged in the weeks of stormy ocean travel and was hard to read. Brigham Young thought it said that Joseph was not happy the members of the Twelve had gone forward to publish the Book of Mormon without his explicit permission. The news grieved Brigham, who had thought he was doing the right thing.

On December 15, however, Joseph wrote the Twelve again, having received a copy of the hymnbook they had printed. Joseph expressed his approval. “I am informed that the Book of Mormon is likewise printed,” he added, “which I am glad to hear, and should be pleased to hear that it was printed in all the different Languages of the earth. You can use your own pleasure respecting printing the Book of Doctrine & Covenants, if there is a great demand for them, I have not any objections, but would rather encourage it.”

Now the members of the Quorum of the Twelve had explicit permission to publish the Doctrine and Covenants in England. Within a few months, however, most of the Twelve were on their way home to Nauvoo, where John Taylor and Wilford Woodruff would play a major role in publishing the Doctrine and Covenants there.

After Joseph Smith was killed in 1844, the Saints assembled in Nauvoo voted that the Quorum of the Twelve, not Sidney Rigdon, was the Prophet’s proper successor. Later that year, Wilford Woodruff left again for England to take charge of printing and emigration for the Church. In February 1845, he wrote, “We shall probably publish the book of ‘Doctrine and Covenants’ in England, as soon as circumstances will permit.”

On March 1, Wilford Woodruff’s thirty-eighth birthday, a letter spurred him to even quicker action. That day, he learned that John Greenhow, a follower of Sidney Rigdon, planned to copyright the Doctrine and Covenants in England to keep the Twelve from doing so. “This certainly is a bold move for an apostate or apostates to undertake to Print the works of the Church & rob them out of it,” Elder Woodruff wrote. “I view it nothing more than the mercy of God in Putting the knowledge of this thing into my hands.” He spent the day researching the copyright law.

The next day, a Sunday, he and his counselors in the mission presidency “called upon God our heavenly Father by prayer in unity for Him to bless us & frustrate the designs of our enemies & overthrow the plans which they have lade to rob the Church and injure the cause of God.”

Elder Woodruff went to work and soon contracted with a printer for three thousand copies of the Doctrine and Covenants. This Liverpool edition was at press by mid-April. He waited eagerly for the last sheets to come from the printer, and within forty-eight hours of getting them, he visited Stationers’ Hall in London to secure the copyright, thereby foiling Greenhow’s plan.
The 1845 Liverpool edition reprinted the text of the 1844 Nauvoo edition, and for the next quarter-century, the Nauvoo text of the Doctrine and Covenants would remain the standard for new printings.

After his success in printing the Doctrine and Covenants and securing a copyright for it, Wilford Woodruff printed a report of his labors in the Millennial Star, the Church’s official organ in England. “I had . . . the pleasing reflection of knowing that I had, upon this 7th day of June, A.D. 1845, the pleasure of securing unto the church the copyright of the Book of Doctrine and Covenants,” he wrote. This work of scripture, he explained, was “brought forth by the mouth of the prophet, seer, and revelator, Joseph Smith,” and was “one of the most important records ever presented to this or any other generation.” He described the intrigue that led to its publication and extended a universal invitation: “We say unto all come and buy, and read for yourselves, digest it, learn wisdom, and practice holiness.”
